turns.content: I'm building a side project where I need to store custom structs in a BTreeSet and then sort them by a specific field using the standard library's sort_by with Ordering. My assumption is that if I just implement Ord for my struct, the BTreeSet will automatically order elements based on any field I want without needing extra logic.

That's a common misconception. Implementing Ord gives you a total ordering based on all fields in your struct, not just one specific field like the one you mentioned. The BTreeSet uses that full Ord implementation for its internal tree structure. If you want to order by only a single field, you cannot rely on implementing Ord alone; instead, you must use sort_by with Ordering or implement PartialOrd and compare exactly the fields you care about within your custom comparison logic.

I see now that I can't just pick one field to be the key. But what if my struct has a method like get_priority() that returns an i32? Can I use sort_by with Ordering and a closure that calls this method, or does BTreeSet require me to implement Ord directly on the struct itself?

BTreeSet requires elements to be comparable via Ord, so it cannot accept arbitrary closures for sorting like sort_by does. That function works with Vec or other collections where you provide a comparator. For BTreeSet, if your struct has a method like get_priority(), you must either implement PartialOrd based on that priority field directly in the trait implementation, or extract the elements into a vector, use sort_by with Ordering and your closure to sort them manually, then insert the sorted results back into your collection.

Hey, I've been reading about implementing Ord and using BTreeSet in my Rust book exercises. I get the basic syntax but still don't fully understand how it actually affects performance when sorting large collections.

That's a great question. Think of Ord like a universal ruler that every item needs to carry to be placed in order. If your data structure doesn't have this ruler, algorithms like BTreeSet can't organize items efficiently because they rely on comparing values directly without needing extra steps.
So I'm confused about why my current approach with Vec and manual comparisons feels slower. Is it just a performance issue?

You're right, there's definitely a performance angle here. When you implement Ord for your struct using traits like PartialEq and PartialOrd, BTreeSet can use binary search to find insertions in O(log n) time instead of the O(n) scan that happens with unsorted collections or sorting algorithms.
Wait, I thought you said we shouldn't implement Ord for structs? That's what I read somewhere about avoiding it.

Actually, that was a misconception. You can and should implement Ord when you need predictable ordering behavior, especially with BTreeSet which requires total ordering to maintain its balanced tree structure without needing to rebuild the tree every time an element is added.
Okay so I need to make sure my struct implements Ord correctly. What exactly does that entail?

You'll use the derive macro for PartialOrd and then manually implement Ord by delegating to a comparison method like cmp on fields you care about most, ensuring your implementation follows the total ordering requirements.
But what if I only want partial ordering? Can BTreeSet handle that?

No, BTreeSet requires total ordering for all elements to maintain its sorted property. If you only need partial ordering or different sort criteria, use Vec with sort_by and the Ordering trait instead.
Got it. So I should avoid Ord when I don't need full ordering? Is that right?
Exactly, but remember to check if your data structure can benefit from the O(log n) insertion and lookup performance of BTreeSet versus the O(n log n) sorting overhead you'd face with Vec.
